title: Mustard on Silk — Glycerin + Minimal Detergent
stainChemistry: Mustard is ground mustard seeds (fat + protein) plus turmeric (dye). Fat component requires solvent; dye component responds to acid/glycerin. Silk cannot tolerate bleach or alkaline. Glycerin mobilizes both components while remaining gentle on protein fiber.
whyThisWorks: Glycerin (amphoteric) dissolves both fat and dye components. Minimal detergent encapsulates residue. No bleach risk on silk.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply glycerin from back side sparingly. Do NOT saturate silk. Allow 10-15 minutes. Blot gently as mustard mobilizes.

spottingProtocol.agent: Blot away glycerin and mustard residue
spottingProtocol.instruction: Blot gently. Do not rub. Yellow residue should transfer to cloth.

spottingProtocol.agent: NSD (1 teaspoon NSD in 1 cup cool water, minimal saturation
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply dilute detergent very sparingly. Do NOT saturate. Detergent encapsulates any remaining mustard particles for lifting.
Use barely damp cloth only. Do NOT spray or saturate. Work quickly—silk weakens at saturation.
Lay flat or hang very loosely. Never wring. As it dries, monitor treated area.

homeSolutions: FRESH MUSTARD: Glycerin + detergent works best within 1 hour.
WITHIN HOURS: Still effective up to 6 hours.
OLDER: Professional treatment may be needed.
materialWarnings: NEVER use hot water—spreads stain and weakens silk.
escalation: If glycerin and detergent don't fully resolve mustard, call professional. Do not attempt bleach or aggressive treatments.

household.name: Food-Grade Glycerin
household.note: Primary treatment. Apply minimally. Mobilizes both fat and dye.

household.name: Seventh Generation Free & Clear (diluted)
household.note: For final residue. Use very diluted. Minimal application.

professional.name: Specialty Silk Treatment
professional.note: For aged or stubborn mustard on silk.

customerExplanation: Mustard is fat plus turmeric dye. Glycerin mobilizes both components without risking bleach or alkaline damage to your delicate silk. This is a gentle multi-step approach.
scienceNote: Mustard's fat component has both hydrophobic and hydrophilic regions. Glycerin (amphipathic—both polar and nonpolar) can mobilize these through polarity matching. The turmeric dye similarly benefits from glycerin's dual-polarity nature. This is why glycerin is ideal—it addresses both components simultaneously without chemical damage risk.
callPro: If glycerin and detergent don't fully resolve mustard, call professional. Silk's delicate nature limits aggressive treatment options.
solventNote: Professional shops: Specialty silk spotting solutions safer than glycerin. Minimal saturation essential. Mustard is two-part stain (fat + dye)—address both components. Do not use bleach or alkaline. Professional expertise prevents fiber damage.
